subject: Mathematics

unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 15. In this unit you will learn how to measure the world in three different ways: length (how far), area (how much surface a flat shape covers) and volume (how much space a solid object fills). These three ideas sit behind an enormous amount of real work. When Hassan helps his uncle pour a concrete floor for a new shop in Hargeisa, he needs volume. When Amina works out how much paint to buy for the walls of the classroom at Nuur Academy, she needs area. When Yusuf plans the fencing around a goat enclosure, he needs length. By the end of this unit you will be able to move between all three with confidence.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, activities and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured from the Cambridge Lower Secondary Mathematics 0862 — Stage 8 workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: Calculate the area of rectangles, triangles, parallelograms and trapezia.
Find the circumference and area of circles, semicircles and quarter-circles using π.
Break a compound shape into simple parts and find its area by addition or subtraction.
Describe the properties (faces, edges, vertices) of common 3D solids.
Calculate the volume of cuboids, prisms and cylinders.
Calculate the surface area of cuboids, cylinders and triangular prisms.
Convert between metric units of length, area and volume correctly.
Use estimation to check that a measurement answer is reasonable.

concepts.title: Area of 2D Shapes
concepts.explanation: Area is the amount of flat surface a shape covers, and it is always measured in square units. Before we can tackle circles and compound shapes, you must be fluent with the four basic area formulas. Learn them until they are automatic. Rectangle: Area = length × width, written A = lw.
concepts.example: Square: Area = side × side, written A = s².

concepts.title: Circles – Circumference and Area
concepts.explanation: A circle is different from the straight-sided shapes, so it needs its own special number: π (pi). Pi is the ratio you get when you divide the distance around any circle (the circumference) by the distance straight across it (the diameter). Amazingly, this ratio is the same for every circle in the world, large or small. Its value is 3.14159…, a number whose digits never end and never repeat. For calculations we usually use 3.14, or the π button on a calculator, or sometimes the fraction 22/7. First learn the parts…
concepts.example: Circumference can be found two ways, and they are really the same formula: C = πd (pi times the diameter) or C = 2πr (pi times twice the radius). Take a circular water tank with radius 4 cm. Its circumference is 2 × π ×…

concepts.title: Compound Shapes
concepts.explanation: A compound shape (also called a composite shape) is made by joining simple shapes together. Almost any real object – the floor plan of a house, a running track, a garden with a pond – is a compound shape. There are two strategies for finding the area, and choosing the right one makes the work easy. Strategy 1 – Addition. Split the shape into simple pieces that do not overlap, find the area of each piece, then add them together. Consider an L-shaped room. You can cut it into two rectangles. If the first rectangle…
concepts.example: Strategy 2 – Subtraction. Start with a large simple shape and cut a hole out of it. Find the area of the big shape, find the area of the hole, then subtract. Imagine a rectangular metal plate 10 cm by 8 cm with a…

concepts.title: 3D Shapes and Their Properties
concepts.explanation: Now we step from flat shapes into three dimensions. A 3D shape (a solid) has length, width and height. Three words describe its structure. A face is a flat surface of the solid. An edge is the line where two faces meet. A vertex is a corner point where edges meet (the plural is vertices). A net is a flat pattern that folds up to make the solid – a very useful idea when we come to surface area. Here are the solids you must know for this unit, with their properties:
concepts.example: A prism is a special and very important kind of solid: it has the same cross-section all the way along its length. If you sliced a triangular prism anywhere along its length, every slice would be the identical triangle.…

concepts.title: Volume of Prisms and Cylinders
concepts.explanation: Volume is the amount of space a solid fills, measured in cubic units such as cm³ and m³. Picture stacking unit cubes to fill the solid; the number of cubes is the volume. For a cuboid, volume is length × width × height: V = lwh. A cuboid box 8 cm by 5 cm by 3 cm holds 8 × 5 × 3 = 120 cm³. For a cube, all three edges are equal, so V = s³. A cube of side 4 cm has volume 4³ = 4 × 4 × 4 = 64 cm³.
concepts.example: The truly powerful idea is the general prism formula. Because a prism has the same cross-section all the way along, its volume is simply the area of the cross-section multiplied by the length (or height): V = area of…

concepts.title: Surface Area of Prisms and Cylinders
concepts.explanation: The surface area of a solid is the total area of all its faces added together. Because it is an area, it is always measured in square units (cm², m²) – never cubic units. A useful way to think about surface area is to imagine unfolding the solid into its flat net; the surface area is the area of that whole net. This is the amount of card you would need to build the box, or the amount of paint to cover the outside. For a cuboid with length l, width w and height h, there are three pairs of matching rectangular…
concepts.example: For a cube, all six faces are identical squares, so SA = 6s². A cube of side 5 cm has surface area 6 × 5² = 6 × 25 = 150 cm².

methods.title: How to find the area of a compound shape
methods.example: A trapezium has parallel sides of 9 cm and 15 cm, with a perpendicular height of 6 cm. Find its area.
steps: Sketch the shape and mark on every length you know.
Decide whether to add pieces together or subtract a hole.
Split the shape into simple parts and label each one.
Find each simple area separately, showing your working.
Combine them and write the answer with square units.

methods.title: How to find the volume of a prism or cylinder
methods.example: A circular pond has a diameter of 14 m. Find its circumference and its area.
steps: Identify the cross-section (the shape of the end face).
Find the area of that cross-section.
Multiply by the length (or height) of the prism.
Write the answer in cubic units, and convert to litres if needed.

methods.title: How to convert units safely
methods.example: A rectangular garden is 12 m by 8 m. A circular pond of radius 2 m sits inside it. Find the area left for planting.
steps: For length, multiply going to smaller units, divide going to larger.
For area, square the factor (1 m² = 10 000 cm²).
For volume, cube the factor (1 m³ = 1 000 000 cm³).
Remember 1 litre = 1000 cm³ and 1 ml = 1 cm³.

methods.title: Volume of a cylinder
methods.example: A cylindrical tin of tomatoes has radius 4 cm and height 10 cm. Find its volume and its capacity in millilitres.
steps: Solution: V = πr²h = π × 4² × 10 = π × 16 × 10 = 160π ≈ 502.65 cm³.
Since 1 cm³ = 1 ml, the capacity is about 502.65 ml.
First find the circular end area (πr² = 16π), then multiply by the height.

methods.title: Surface area of a cuboid
methods.example: Find the surface area of a cuboid measuring 10 cm by 6 cm by 4 cm.
steps: Find the three different face areas, add them, then double for the matching pairs.
Check the surface area of a cuboid result carefully.

methods.title: Volume of a triangular prism
methods.example: A triangular prism has a triangular cross-section with base 6 cm and height 5 cm. The prism is 12 cm long. Find its volume.
steps: Solution: First the cross-section area: A = ½ × 6 × 5 = 15 cm².
Then multiply by the length: V = 15 × 12 = 180 cm³.
This is the prism rule: area of cross-section times length.

workedExamples.title: Area of a trapezium
workedExamples.prompt: A trapezium has parallel sides of 9 cm and 15 cm, with a perpendicular height of 6 cm. Find its area.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Use A = ½(a + b)h. Add the parallel sides: 9 + 15 = 24. Halve: ½ × 24 = 12. Multiply by the height: 12 × 6 = 72 cm². Always add the parallel sides first, then halve, then multiply by the height.

workedExamples.title: Circumference and area of a circle
workedExamples.prompt: A circular pond has a diameter of 14 m. Find its circumference and its area.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The radius is half the diameter: r = 14 ÷ 2 = 7 m. Circumference: C = πd = π × 14 = 14π ≈ 43.98 m. Area: A = πr² = π × 7² = 49π ≈ 153.94 m². Notice we halved the diameter before using the area formula, and we squared the radius, not π.

workedExamples.title: Compound shape by subtraction
workedExamples.prompt: A rectangular garden is 12 m by 8 m. A circular pond of radius 2 m sits inside it. Find the area left for planting.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Rectangle area = 12 × 8 = 96 m². Pond area = π × 2² = 4π ≈ 12.57 m². Planting area = 96 − 12.57 = 83.43 m². We subtracted because the pond is a hole cut out of the garden.

workedExamples.title: Volume of a cylinder
workedExamples.prompt: A cylindrical tin of tomatoes has radius 4 cm and height 10 cm. Find its volume and its capacity in millilitres.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: V = πr²h = π × 4² × 10 = π × 16 × 10 = 160π ≈ 502.65 cm³. Since 1 cm³ = 1 ml, the capacity is about 502.65 ml. First find the circular end area (πr² = 16π), then multiply by the height.

workedExamples.title: Surface area of a cuboid
workedExamples.prompt: Find the surface area of a cuboid measuring 10 cm by 6 cm by 4 cm.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: SA = 2(lw + lh + wh) = 2(10×6 + 10×4 + 6×4) = 2(60 + 40 + 24) = 2 × 124 = 248 cm². Find the three different face areas, add them, then double for the matching pairs.

workedExamples.title: Volume of a triangular prism
workedExamples.prompt: A triangular prism has a triangular cross-section with base 6 cm and height 5 cm. The prism is 12 cm long. Find its volume.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: First the cross-section area: A = ½ × 6 × 5 = 15 cm². Then multiply by the length: V = 15 × 12 = 180 cm³. This is the prism rule: area of cross-section times length.

workedExamples.title: Area unit conversion
workedExamples.prompt: Convert 3 m² into cm², and convert 7500 cm² into m².
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Since 1 m² = 10 000 cm², we multiply: 3 × 10 000 = 30 000 cm². Going the other way we divide: 7500 ÷ 10 000 = 0.75 m². Remember to square the factor 100 to get 10 000 – this is the step students most often miss.

workedExamples.title: A real-world tank problem
workedExamples.prompt: Zahra designs a cylindrical water tank with radius 0.5 m and height 2 m. How many litres does it hold?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: V = πr²h = π × 0.5² × 2 = π × 0.25 × 2 = 0.5π ≈ 1.571 m³. Convert to litres: 1 m³ = 1000 litres, so 1.571 × 1000 ≈ 1571 litres. Keeping the radius in metres gave the volume directly in cubic metres, ready to change into litres.

workedExamples.title: Practice 9
workedExamples.prompt: Find the surface area of a cuboid 8 cm by 5 cm by 3 cm.

workedExamples.title: Practice 10
workedExamples.prompt: An L-shape is made of a 10 cm by 6 cm rectangle with a 4 cm by 3 cm rectangle removed from one corner. Find its area.

workedExamples.title: Practice 11
workedExamples.prompt: Convert: a) 3.5 m to cm b) 4 m² to cm² c) 2000 cm³ to litres.

workedExamples.title: Practice 12
workedExamples.prompt: A rectangle 14 cm by 9 cm has a circular hole of radius 3 cm cut out. Find the remaining area.

activities.title: Measure Your Home
activities.materials: a tape measure or ruler, paper, a calculator.
steps: Choose a rectangular room. Measure its length and width in metres.
Calculate the floor area in square metres (A = length × width).
Measure the height of the walls and calculate the total wall area for painting (remember four walls).
Subtract the area of any door and window. How much wall would actually need paint?
If paint covers 10 m² per litre, how many litres would you need?

activities.title: The π Investigation
activities.materials: several round objects (a tin, a cup, a plate), string, a ruler.
steps: Wrap the string once around each object to measure its circumference, then measure it against the ruler.
Measure the diameter straight across each object.
For each object, divide the circumference by the diameter. Write down your answers.
What do you notice? All your answers should be close to 3.14 – you have discovered π for yourself!
Explain why your numbers are not exactly 3.14159. (Hint: think about measuring errors.)

activities.title: Build a Net, Find the Surface Area
activities.materials: card or thick paper, scissors, a ruler, tape.
steps: Draw the net of a cuboid: six rectangles that fold into a closed box. Choose your own dimensions.
Before folding, calculate the surface area by adding the area of all six rectangles.
Cut out and fold the net into the box to check it works.
Now calculate the volume of your box (length × width × height).
Compare two boxes with the same volume but different shapes. Which uses less card (less surface area)?

activities.title: Volume and Capacity Challenge
activities.materials: a cylindrical container (tin or bottle), a ruler, a measuring jug, water.
steps: Measure the radius and height of the container in centimetres.
Calculate its volume using V = πr²h. Convert to millilitres (1 cm³ = 1 ml).
Now fill the container with water and pour it into a measuring jug to read the real capacity.
Compare your calculated capacity with the measured one. How close were they?
Explain any difference. (Hint: was the container filled right to the brim? Is it a perfect cylinder?)

activities.title: Compound Shape Treasure Map (Family Game)
activities.materials: graph paper, a pencil, a ruler.
steps: On graph paper, draw an interesting compound shape (an L-shape, a house shape, or a shape with a semicircle).
Label all the lengths you would need to find the area.
Challenge a family member to calculate the area by splitting it into simple pieces.
Swap shapes and check each other's working. Did you both split it the same way?
Discuss: is it easier to add pieces or subtract a hole for your shape?

activities.title: The Packaging Design Investigation
activities.materials: paper, a calculator, an object to package (a small box or tin).
steps: Choose a fixed volume, for example 1000 cm³.
Find three different cuboid designs that all hold exactly 1000 cm³ (for example 10×10×10, 20×10×5, 25×8×5).
Calculate the surface area of each design.
Which design uses the least material? What does that tell you about efficient packaging?
Write a short recommendation to a company about the best box shape and why.

rules.title: The truly powerful idea is the general
rules.text: The truly powerful idea is the general prism formula. Because a prism has the same cross-section all the way along, its volume is simply the area of the cross-section multiplied by the length (or height): V = area of cross-section × length. This one rule covers every prism. For a triangular prism whose triangular cross-section has area ½ × 6 × 4 = 12 cm² and whose length is 10 cm, the volume is 12 × 10 = 120 cm³. First find the area of the end face, then multiply by how long the prism is – that is all there is to it.

rules.title: A cylinder follows exactly the same idea
rules.text: A cylinder follows exactly the same idea. Its cross-section is a circle of area πr², and its length is the height h, so its volume is V = πr²h. Take a cylindrical can with radius 3 cm and height 8 cm. Its volume is π × 3² × 8 = π × 9 × 8 = 72π ≈ 226.19 cm³. Notice the pattern: find the area of the circular end (πr²), then multiply by the height. It is the prism rule again, wearing circular clothes.

rules.title: You have now met seven big ideas
rules.text: You have now met seven big ideas: the areas of straight-sided shapes, circles with π, compound shapes, the properties of solids, the volume of prisms and cylinders, the surface area of solids, and unit conversions. These ideas are not separate – real problems often use several at once. When Ibrahim designs a cylindrical water tank for the masjid courtyard, he uses circle area to find the cross-section, the prism rule to find the volume, capacity units to turn cm³ into litres, and surface area to work out how much metal sheet to buy. One object, four ideas from this unit, all working together.

rules.title: Solution: First the cross-section area: A =
rules.text: Solution: First the cross-section area: A = ½ × 6 × 5 = 15 cm². Then multiply by the length: V = 15 × 12 = 180 cm³. This is the prism rule: area of cross-section times length.

rules.title: In the next unit you will build
rules.text: In the next unit you will build on these measurement skills, often combining them with algebra and ratio. Before you move on, make sure the foundations here are firm. Prerequisite check: Can you find the area of a circle from its radius? Can you use the prism rule V = cross-section × length? Can you convert m² to cm² by squaring the factor? Can you tell surface area from volume by their units? If yes, you are ready. If any feel shaky, spend a little longer here and ask your AI tutor to review that skill with you.

terms: Flat surface a 2D shape covers (square units)
Centre to edge / across through centre (d = 2r)
A shape built from simpler shapes
Total area of all faces of a solid
Amount a container holds (1 litre = 1000 cm³)
commonMistakes: Halve the diameter first: r = d ÷ 2
Surface area is in cm², volume in cm³
Always write cm, cm² or cm³

realProblems.prompt: A rectangular field in Garissa is 40 m by 25 m. Fencing costs 8 shillings per metre and turf costs 5 shillings per square metre. Find the total cost of fencing the perimeter and turfing the whole field.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

realProblems.prompt: Omar stores camel milk in a cylindrical churn of radius 20 cm and height 60 cm. How many litres does it hold? (Give your answer to the nearest litre.)
realProblems.answer: = 130 m, fencing = 130 × 8 = 1040 sh; area = 40 × 25 = 1000 m², turf = 1000 × 5 = 5000 sh; total = 6040 shillings.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: = 130 m, fencing = 130 × 8 = 1040 sh; area = 40 × 25 = 1000 m², turf = 1000 × 5 = 5000 sh; total = 6040 shillings.

A shape is a rectangle 10 cm by 6 cm with a semicircle (diameter 6 cm) on one short end. Find the total area.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Rectangle 10 × 6 = 60; semicircle radius 3, area ½ × π × 3² = 4.5π ≈ 14.14; total ≈ 74.14 cm².

questions.question: What does “Area” mean?
options: Flat surface a 2D shape covers (square units)
Centre to edge / across through centre (d = 2r)
questions.answer: Flat surface a 2D shape covers (square units)
questions.hint: Use the Unit 15 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Area means Flat surface a 2D shape covers (square units).

questions.question: What does “Perimeter” mean?
options: Centre to edge / across through centre (d = 2r)
questions.hint: Use the Unit 15 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Perimeter means Total distance around a shape.

questions.question: What does “Circumference” mean?
options: Centre to edge / across through centre (d = 2r)
A shape built from simpler shapes
questions.hint: Use the Unit 15 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Circumference means The perimeter of a circle.

selfAssessment: I can calculate the area of rectangles, triangles, parallelograms and trapezia.
I can find the circumference and area of circles, semicircles and quarter-circles using π.
I can break a compound shape into simple parts and find its area by addition or subtraction.
I can describe the properties (faces, edges, vertices) of common 3D solids.
I can calculate the volume of cuboids, prisms and cylinders.
I can calculate the surface area of cuboids, cylinders and triangular prisms.
I can convert between metric units of length, area and volume correctly.
I can use estimation to check that a measurement answer is reasonable.
